message.content:
You are verifier #1, independent. Do NOT optimistically confirm. Read the ACTUAL code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and independently determine whether this is a TRUE production-readiness problem in the CURRENT code (3 hardening passes already applied).

Finding G-017: Unit-level confirm validates predicted_direction shape but never uses it, leaving direction_supports_claim unverified
File: src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:162-168
Description: confirm_unit_level re-validates that hypothesis.predicted_direction is in {'greater','less'} as defense-in-depth, but the unit-level verdict is decided entirely by the trusted caller flag direction_supports_claim and never cross-checked against the pre-registered predicted_direction. A hypothesis locked with predicted_direction='greater' can be confirmed 'supported' on direction_supports_claim=True even when the held-out statistic points the other way — the engine binds a direction into the proof then ignores it at decision time. The validation at lines 162-168 gives a false sense the locked direction governs the verdict; it does not. Smaller than G-001 (requires a dishonest caller flag rather than free post-hoc choice) but the locked direction provides zero decision-time guarantee for the unit-level path.
PoC: Hypothesis locked predicted_direction='greater'. Caller passes direction_supports_claim=True with a statistic actually lower than baseline. verdict_from_pvalue returns SUPPORTED purely on the flag; the locked 'greater' is never consulted.
Suggested fix: Require the caller to pass the observed comparison sign and derive supports = (observed_relation == locked_direction) inside the engine rather than accepting a free boolean. At minimum, when predicted_direction is locked, validate direction_supports_claim is consistent with caller-supplied directional evidence and record both in the verdict.

Confirmed=true ONLY if the problem exists as described in current code, the PoC is reproducible, and it is a genuine concern (not speculative, not already-handled, not intended design). Be concrete and cite file:line.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll independently verify this finding by reading the actual code.

content.content:
1	"""Confirmatory-test machinery (METHODOLOGY.md §2, §4).
2	
3	A confirmatory test takes a *locked* hypothesis and returns a :class:`Verdict`. Two
4	mechanisms, selected by hypothesis kind:
5	
6	- unit-level → evaluate on a held-out confirmation partition (Firewall B via
7	  :class:`DiscoverConfirmSplit`);
8	- whole-corpus → compare the observed statistic against a null/permutation distribution and
9	  require stability across resamples.
10	
11	This module defines the *contract and guards* around confirmation. The concrete statistics
12	are supplied by callers via the baseline toolkit; the engine's responsibility here is to
13	refuse to confirm an unlocked hypothesis or on discovery data, validate the supplied
14	statistics, and map an outcome onto one of the three verdicts honestly (including
15	``indeterminate``).
16	
17	Direction handling (audit pass 1, issue #2): a claim predicts the statistic lies in a
18	particular tail of the null. The empirical p-value is computed against *that* tail, so a
19	claim predicting a low value is confirmable, and a genuine contradiction (significant in the
20	opposite tail) is detectable — both were impossible under the previous direction-blind
21	upper-tail-only implementation.
22	"""
23	
24	from __future__ import annotations
25	
26	import datetime as _dt
27	import math
28	from typing import Iterable, Sequence
29	
30	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import DiscoverConfirmSplit, FirewallViolation
31	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Direction, Hypothesis, HypothesisKind
32	from assay_engine.methodology.preregistration import (
33	    TimestampAuthority,
34	    require_preregistered,
35	)
36	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict
37	
38	
39	def _gate_preregistration(hypothesis: Hypothesis, authority: TimestampAuthority | None) -> None:
40	    """Pre-registration gate for the confirm primitives.
41	
42	    If ``authority`` is supplied, run the full :func:`require_preregistered` check (content
43	    binding + verifiable timestamp + lock-before-this-moment) — so a study calling a confirm
44	    primitive *directly* (outside a runner) gets the same guarantee the runners enforce. If it
45	    is ``None``, fall back to the cheap presence gate (:func:`require_locked`); the caller has
46	    then opted out of real verification and owns that choice.
47	    """
48	    if authority is not None:
49	        require_preregistered(
50	            hypothesis, authority=authority, not_after=_dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc)
51	        )
52	    else:
53	        require_locked(hypothesis)
54	
55	
56	def require_locked(hypothesis: Hypothesis) -> None:
57	    """Cheap **presence** gate: refuse a hypothesis that carries no lock fields at all.
58	
59	    This is defense-in-depth, not the methodology-grade check. It does not verify the proof
60	    binds the content or that the lock precedes confirmation — that is
61	    :func:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.require_preregistered`, which the
62	    confirmatory *runners* (:mod:`adjudication`, :mod:`discovery`) call before they confirm.
63	    A study driving :func:`confirm_whole_corpus`/:func:`confirm_unit_level` directly (outside a
64	    runner) should call ``require_preregistered`` itself to get the real guarantee.
65	    """
66	    if not hypothesis.locked:
67	        raise FirewallViolation(
68	            f"hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} is not locked; confirm is forbidden "
69	            "before pre-registration (Firewall B / pre-registration)"
70	        )
71	
72	
73	def _validate_alpha(alpha: float) -> None:
74	    # A significance threshold of 0.5 or more is not a meaningful one-sided level, and
75	    # admitting it lets both tails register "significant" at once (alpha < 0.5 guarantees
76	    # p_support + p_contra >= 1 cannot both fall below alpha) — fix-review nit on issue #2.
77	    if not (0.0 < alpha < 0.5):
78	        raise ValueError(f"alpha must be in (0, 0.5); got {alpha}")
79	
80	
81	def _validate_pvalue(p_value: float) -> None:
82	    if not (0.0 <= p_value <= 1.0):
83	        raise ValueError(f"p_value must be in [0, 1]; got {p_value}")
84	
85	
86	def _validate_finite(name: str, value: float) -> None:
87	    if not math.isfinite(value):
88	        raise ValueError(f"{name} must be a finite number; got {value}")
89	
90	
91	def verdict_from_pvalue(
92	    hypothesis_id: str,
93	    *,
94	    statistic: float,
95	    p_value: float,
96	    alpha: float,
97	    powered: bool,
98	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
99	    test_description: str = "confirmatory test",
100	) -> Verdict:
101	    """Map a confirmatory statistic onto the three verdicts.
102	
103	    - not ``powered`` → ``indeterminate`` (the method cannot decide).
104	    - significant and direction agrees with the claim → ``supported``.
105	    - significant and direction disagrees → ``contradicted``.
106	    - not significant → ``indeterminate`` (absence of evidence is not contradiction here).
107	
108	    ``p_value`` and ``direction_supports_claim`` must be derived by the caller from a test
109	    appropriate to the claim's predicted direction. For a unit-level held-out test the engine
110	    is handed only an opaque ``statistic`` + ``p_value`` and cannot itself infer whether the
111	    statistic supports or contradicts the claim — so ``direction_supports_claim`` is a trusted
112	    caller obligation, ``statistic`` is recorded for provenance only (it does NOT gate the
113	    verdict), and the deciding flag is recorded in ``evidence`` so the verdict is re-derivable
114	    (audit pass 2, issue #20). Inputs are validated (issue #4) and the recorded decision rule
115	    reflects the caller's ``test_description``, not a hard-coded sidedness label (issue #2).
116	    """
117	    _validate_pvalue(p_value)
118	    _validate_alpha(alpha)
119	    _validate_finite("statistic", statistic)
120	
121	    rule = f"{test_description} at alpha={alpha}; powered={powered}"
122	    common = dict(
123	        statistic=statistic,
124	        threshold=alpha,
125	        evidence={"p_value": p_value, "direction_supports_claim": direction_supports_claim},
126	    )
127	    if not powered:
128	        return Verdict.indeterminate(hypothesis_id, rule, notes="underpowered", **common)
129	    if p_value <= alpha:
130	        if direction_supports_claim:
131	            return Verdict.supported(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
132	        return Verdict.contradicted(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
133	    return Verdict.indeterminate(hypothesis_id, rule, notes="not significant at alpha", **common)
134	
135	
136	def confirm_unit_level(
137	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
138	    *,
139	    split: DiscoverConfirmSplit,
140	    evaluated_ids: Iterable[str],
141	    statistic: float,
142	    p_value: float,
143	    alpha: float,
144	    powered: bool,
145	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
146	    authority: TimestampAuthority | None = None,
147	) -> Verdict:
148	    """Confirm a unit-level hypothesis on the held-out partition only.
149	
150	    Pass ``authority`` to verify pre-registration in full (content binding + timestamp +
151	    lock-before-now); omit it to fall back to the presence gate (see :func:`require_locked`).
152	    """
153	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.UNIT_LEVEL:
154	        raise ValueError("confirm_unit_level requires a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis")
155	    # Defense-in-depth direction validation (pass 3, #F-002): the unit-level path takes the
156	    # deciding direction as a trusted caller flag (``direction_supports_claim``) and so never
157	    # routed through ``_resolve_direction`` — which left a locked-but-invalid
158	    # ``predicted_direction`` (e.g. "UP") entirely unchecked here. ``Hypothesis.__post_init__``
159	    # now rejects such values at construction, but re-validate any pre-registered direction here
160	    # so a hypothesis constructed by an unusual path (or a future field mutation) cannot confirm
161	    # against an unrecognized tail.
162	    if hypothesis.predicted_direction is not None and (
163	        hypothesis.predicted_direction not in _VALID_DIRECTIONS
164	    ):
165	        raise ValueError(
166	            f"predicted_direction must be one of {_VALID_DIRECTIONS}; got "
167	            f"{hypothesis.predicted_direction!r}"
168	        )
169	    _gate_preregistration(hypothesis, authority)
170	    split.assert_confirm_only(evaluated_ids)  # Firewall B (rejects empty / discovery ids)
171	    return verdict_from_pvalue(
172	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
173	        statistic=statistic,
174	        p_value=p_value,
175	        alpha=alpha,
176	        powered=powered,
177	        direction_supports_claim=direction_supports_claim,
178	        test_description="held-out unit-level test",
179	    )
180	
181	
182	def _empirical_p(null: Sequence[float], observed: float, tail: Direction) -> float:
183	    """One-sided empirical p-value with +1 smoothing for the given tail."""
184	    n = len(null)
185	    if tail == "greater":
186	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v >= observed)
187	    else:
188	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v <= observed)
189	    return (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
190	
191	
192	def _resample_stability(
193	    resamples: Sequence[float], null: Sequence[float], tail: Direction, alpha: float
194	) -> float:
195	    """Fraction of resamples that **reproduce the observed effect**.
196	
197	    Each resample is scored as its own observed statistic against the null and must itself be
198	    significant in the predicted tail (empirical p ≤ ``alpha``). This measures reproduction of
199	    the observed *effect* — not merely landing on the predicted side of the null center, which a
200	    resample distribution clearing the null median by an epsilon would satisfy while reproducing
201	    nothing of the observed magnitude (audit pass 2, #139). The closer to 1.0, the more
202	    consistently the effect's significance reproduces across resamples.
203	
204	    The default path is O(R·N) pure Python (one null pass per resample). When numpy is available
205	    (the optional ``baseline`` extra) the whole R×N comparison runs as a single vectorized op
206	    (pass 3, #F-017). The numpy path computes the IDENTICAL integer extreme-counts and the same
207	    ``(extreme + 1) / (n + 1) <= alpha`` test, so its result is exactly equal to the Python path;
208	    it is purely a speed optimization for large R·N, never a semantic change.
209	    """
210	    n = len(null)
211	    try:
212	        import numpy as np
213	
214	        null_arr = np.asarray(null, dtype=np.float64)
215	        res_arr = np.asarray(resamples, dtype=np.float64)
216	        if tail == "greater":
217	            extreme = (null_arr[None, :] >= res_arr[:, None]).sum(axis=1)
218	        else:
219	            extreme = (null_arr[None, :] <= res_arr[:, None]).sum(axis=1)
220	        p = (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
221	        return float(int((p <= alpha).sum()) / len(resamples))
222	    except ImportError:
223	        significant = sum(1 for r in resamples if _empirical_p(null, r, tail) <= alpha)
224	        return significant / len(resamples)
225	
226	
227	_VALID_DIRECTIONS = ("greater", "less")
228	
229	
230	def _resolve_direction(hypothesis: Hypothesis, predicted_direction: Direction | None) -> Direction:
231	    """Direction is fixed at lock time on the hypothesis (issue #24); the confirm-time
232	    argument is optional and, if given, must match the pre-registered one.
233	
234	    The resolved direction is validated against ``{"greater", "less"}`` and any other value is
235	    rejected: an unrecognized direction would otherwise fall through to the 'less' tail in
236	    :func:`_empirical_p`, silently flipping supported↔contradicted (audit pass 2, #128).
237	    """
238	    locked_dir = hypothesis.predicted_direction
239	    if locked_dir is not None:
240	        if predicted_direction is not None and predicted_direction != locked_dir:
241	            raise ValueError(
242	                "predicted_direction argument contradicts the hypothesis's pre-registered "
243	                f"direction ({predicted_direction!r} != {locked_dir!r})"
244	            )
245	        resolved = locked_dir
246	    elif predicted_direction is None:
247	        raise ValueError(
248	            "no predicted_direction: set it on the hypothesis at pre-registration "
249	            "(preferred) or pass it explicitly"
250	        )
251	    else:
252	        resolved = predicted_direction
253	    if resolved not in _VALID_DIRECTIONS:
254	        raise ValueError(
255	            f"predicted_direction must be one of {_VALID_DIRECTIONS}; got {resolved!r} — an "
256	            "unrecognized tail would silently flip the supported/contradicted verdict"
257	        )
258	    return resolved
259	
260	
261	def confirm_whole_corpus(
262	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
263	    *,
264	    observed: float,
265	    null_distribution: Sequence[float],
266	    alpha: float,
267	    resample_statistics: Sequence[float] | None = None,
268	    stability_threshold: float = 0.9,
269	    predicted_direction: Direction | None = None,
270	    authority: TimestampAuthority | None = None,
271	) -> Verdict:
272	    """Confirm a whole-corpus hypothesis against a null distribution + measured stability.
273	
274	    Pass ``authority`` to verify pre-registration in full (content binding + timestamp +
275	    lock-before-now); omit it to fall back to the presence gate (see :func:`require_locked`).
276	
277	    The predicted tail is taken from the hypothesis's pre-registered ``predicted_direction``
278	    (issue #24). The pattern must (a) beat the null at ``alpha`` in the predicted tail and
279	    (b) be **stable across resamples** — measured by the engine from ``resample_statistics``,
280	    not asserted by the caller (audit pass 2, issue #21). Outcomes:
281	
282	    - significant in the predicted tail AND stable → ``supported``;
283	    - significant in the *opposite* tail → ``contradicted``;
284	    - unstable, not significant, or stability not assessed → ``indeterminate``.
285	
286	    If ``resample_statistics`` is omitted, stability cannot be measured, so ``supported`` is
287	    unavailable and the verdict is ``indeterminate`` ("stability not assessed").
288	    """
289	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.WHOLE_CORPUS:
290	        raise ValueError("confirm_whole_corpus requires a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis")
291	    _gate_preregistration(hypothesis, authority)
292	    _validate_alpha(alpha)
293	    _validate_finite("observed", observed)
294	    if not null_distribution:
295	        raise ValueError(
296	            "a null/permutation distribution is required for whole-corpus confirmation"
297	        )
298	    for v in null_distribution:
299	        _validate_finite("null_distribution value", v)
300	    if not (0.0 < stability_threshold <= 1.0):
301	        raise ValueError(f"stability_threshold must be in (0, 1]; got {stability_threshold}")
302	
303	    direction = _resolve_direction(hypothesis, predicted_direction)
304	    opposite: Direction = "less" if direction == "greater" else "greater"
305	    p_support = _empirical_p(null_distribution, observed, direction)
306	    p_contra = _empirical_p(null_distribution, observed, opposite)
307	    n = len(null_distribution)
308	
309	    stability: float | None = None
310	    if resample_statistics is not None:
311	        if not resample_statistics:
312	            raise ValueError("resample_statistics, if given, must be non-empty")
313	        for r in resample_statistics:
314	            _validate_finite("resample statistic", r)
315	        stability = _resample_stability(resample_statistics, null_distribution, direction, alpha)
316	    stable = stability is not None and stability >= stability_threshold
317	
318	    rule = (
319	        f"empirical p from {n}-sample null at alpha={alpha}, predicted tail '{direction}'; "
320	        f"stability across resamples >= {stability_threshold} (engine-measured)"
321	    )
322	    evidence = {
323	        "p_support": p_support,
324	        "p_contradict": p_contra,
325	        "null_n": n,
326	        "predicted_direction": direction,
327	        "stability": stability,
328	        "stability_threshold": stability_threshold,
329	        "n_resamples": len(resample_statistics) if resample_statistics is not None else 0,
330	    }
331	    common = dict(statistic=observed, threshold=alpha, evidence=evidence)
332	
333	    # A contradiction (significant in the opposite tail) is reportable without stability.
334	    if p_contra <= alpha:
335	        return Verdict.contradicted(
336	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="significant in the opposite tail", **common
337	        )
338	    if stability is None:
339	        return Verdict.indeterminate(
340	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
341	            rule,
342	            notes="stability not assessed (no resamples supplied)",
343	            **common,
344	        )
345	    if not stable:
346	        return Verdict.indeterminate(
347	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="unstable across resamples", **common
348	        )
349	    if p_support <= alpha:
350	        return Verdict.supported(hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
351	    return Verdict.indeterminate(
352	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="does not beat null at alpha", **common
353	    )
354

content.text:
The finding is accurate. Let me confirm the key facts in the code.

The unit-level path (`confirm_unit_level`, lines 136-179) takes `direction_supports_claim` as a trusted caller boolean and passes it directly to `verdict_from_pvalue`. The validation at lines 162-168 only checks that `predicted_direction` is a recognized string value — it never cross-checks it against `direction_supports_claim`. The verdict at lines 129-133 is decided purely by `p_value <= alpha` and the `direction_supports_claim` flag; `statistic` is "recorded for provenance only (it does NOT gate the verdict)" per the docstring.

Compare to the whole-corpus path, which derives direction from the data: `_empirical_p(null_distribution, observed, direction)` actually computes the tail comparison against the locked direction (lines 303-306). The unit-level path has no such derivation.

So the PoC holds: a hypothesis locked `predicted_direction='greater'` with a caller passing `direction_supports_claim=True` (even when the statistic is actually lower than baseline) yields SUPPORTED. The locked direction provides zero decision-time guarantee for the unit-level path.

However, I need to assess the severity honestly. This is by **explicit, documented design** — the docstring at lines 108-115 states this is a "trusted caller obligation" because "the engine is handed only an opaque `statistic` + `p_value` and cannot itself infer whether the statistic supports or contradicts the claim." Let me check whether there's a contract/discipline already documented around this and whether the engine genuinely cannot do better.

content.content:
src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:98:    direction_supports_claim: bool,
src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:108:    ``p_value`` and ``direction_supports_claim`` must be derived by the caller from a test
src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:111:    statistic supports or contradicts the claim — so ``direction_supports_claim`` is a trusted
src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:125:        evidence={"p_value": p_value, "direction_supports_claim": direction_supports_claim},
src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:130:        if direction_supports_claim:
src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:145:    direction_supports_claim: bool,
src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:156:    # deciding direction as a trusted caller flag (``direction_supports_claim``) and so never
src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:177:        direction_supports_claim=direction_supports_claim,
tests/test_confirm.py:66:            direction_supports_claim=True,
tests/test_confirm.py:77:            direction_supports_claim=False,
tests/test_confirm.py:88:            direction_supports_claim=True,
tests/test_confirm.py:99:            direction_supports_claim=True,
tests/test_confirm.py:115:            direction_supports_claim=True,
tests/test_confirm.py:126:            direction_supports_claim=False,
tests/test_confirm.py:146:        statistic=1.0, p_value=0.01, alpha=0.05, powered=True, direction_supports_claim=True

content.text: Let me check how the unit-level confirm is actually invoked in the runners, to see if direction is cross-checked upstream.

So `confirm_unit_level` is not even wired into a runner that derives the direction — it's a public primitive whose direction correctness rests entirely on the caller. Let me check the related finding G-001 context and whether there's a held-out statistic the engine could compare against a baseline.

The finding accurately describes the situation. Let me make my severity assessment.

**Analysis:**

The technical claim is TRUE and reproducible:
- `confirm_unit_level` accepts `direction_supports_claim: bool` as a trusted caller input (line 145).
- The validation at lines 162-168 only validates the *string membership* of `predicted_direction`, never cross-checking it against `direction_supports_claim`.
- `verdict_from_pvalue` decides SUPPORTED vs CONTRADICTED purely on the flag (lines 129-132); the `statistic` is explicitly provenance-only and does not gate the verdict (lines 112-113, docstring).
- Therefore a hypothesis locked `predicted_direction='greater'` confirmed with `direction_supports_claim=True` returns SUPPORTED regardless of whether the held-out statistic actually points greater. The locked direction is never consulted at decision time on this path.

This contrasts with `confirm_whole_corpus`, which genuinely derives the verdict from the data via `_empirical_p(null, observed, direction)` against the locked direction (lines 303-306). So the asymmetry the finding describes is real.

**Severity calibration (honest, not optimistic):**
This is the documented, intended design of the unit-level primitive: the engine receives "only an opaque `statistic` + `p_value`" and cannot itself infer support/contradiction, so it delegates to the caller as a "trusted caller obligation" (docstring 108-115). It requires a *dishonest or buggy caller* — there is no free post-hoc choice as in G-001. The finding itself acknowledges this ("Smaller than G-001 ... requires a dishonest caller flag"). The suggested fix (have the engine derive `supports = (observed_relation == locked_direction)` from caller-supplied directional evidence) is a genuine, implementable hardening that would close the gap and make the locked direction load-bearing on this path, matching the whole-corpus path's guarantee.

The concern is real and not already-handled, but the integrity impact is bounded: the methodology framework's entire value proposition is that pre-registered commitments (like locked direction) bind the verdict. A path where a locked commitment is decoratively recorded but provides "zero decision-time guarantee" undermines that proposition, but only against a non-honest caller. That makes it a genuine medium-severity hardening gap rather than a critical/high one.

input.reasoning: TRUE and reproducible in current code. confirm_unit_level (src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:136-179) takes direction_supports_claim:bool as a trusted caller input (line 145) and the validation at lines 162-168 only checks predicted_direction is a recognized string in _VALID_DIRECTIONS — it never cross-checks the locked direction against direction_supports_claim. The verdict is decided entirely by p_value<=alpha and the flag in verdict_from_pvalue (lines 129-132); the docstring at lines 112-113 explicitly states statistic is "recorded for provenance only (it does NOT gate the verdict)". So a hypothesis locked predicted_direction='greater' confirmed with direction_supports_claim=True returns SUPPORTED even when the held-out statistic points lower — the PoC holds exactly. The asymmetry the finding cites is real: confirm_whole_corpus genuinely derives the verdict from data via _empirical_p(null, observed, direction) against the locked direction (lines 303-306), giving the locked direction decision-time force, while the unit-level path consults the locked direction only for string-validation, never for the verdict. confirm_unit_level is also a bare public primitive (no runner in discovery.py/adjudication.py derives the direction for it — grep shows only confirm_whole_corpus-style wiring), so correctness rests entirely on the caller flag. Severity is medium, not high: this is the documented intended design (docstring 108-115: "trusted caller obligation" because the engine receives "only an opaque statistic + p_value"), and exploitation requires a dishonest/buggy caller rather than a free post-hoc choice (the finding itself concedes it is smaller than G-001). It is nonetheless a genuine integrity gap — a pre-registered commitment (locked direction) is decoratively recorded but provides zero decision-time guarantee on this path, contradicting the framework's core premise that locks bind verdicts — and the suggested fix (engine derives supports = observed_relation == locked_direction from caller-supplied directional evidence) is implementable and would align the unit-level path with the whole-corpus guarantee.

content.text: Confirmed G-017 as a true medium-severity finding. The unit-level confirm path validates `predicted_direction`'s string form but never binds it to the verdict, which is decided entirely by the trusted `direction_supports_claim` caller flag — making the pre-registered direction decorative at decision time, in contrast to the whole-corpus path which derives the verdict from data against the locked direction.
